text: The surgeon retracts the gallbladder superiorly with a 5mm grasper. A two-row silk suture on a straight needle is passed through the epigastrium and infundibulum of the gallbladder, exiting laterally, enabling manipulation of the gallbladder neck for better exposure.

The surgeon uses a conventional straight hook dissector to incise the peritoneum overlying the neck of the gallbladder, exposing the cystic duct and artery. The posterior peritoneal attachments are also incised, allowing clear visualization of the cystic duct and cystic duct node.
The surgeon dissects the cystic artery free and places conventional five millimeter locking hemo clips across the cystic duct, which is then divided.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to dissect the remaining attachments between the gallbladder and the liver bed in a retrograde fashion. Medial and lateral retraction of the silk suture provides excellent exposure during the dissection.
The surgeon divides the remaining attachments between the gallbladder and liver bed, freeing the gallbladder completely from the liver bed.

The surgeon irrigates the surgical field, then grasps the gallbladder and withdraws it through the umbilical incision. The fascial defect at the umbilicus is closed with interrupted zero PDS sutures, followed by closure of the skin with fine absorbable sutures.
